transcript.transcript: This is Marilyn Shuler. I'm about to interview Howard Kopple in Boise, Idaho, and today is March 2, 2007. Hey Howard. Were you born in Boise? I was born here in 1926 June the 6th. Okay, so do you have some early memories of Julia Davis Park? I have memories of Julia Davis Park and they're all good memories, happy memories. Actually I used to come here as a four and five and six year old to watch my dad play tennis, because he was a tennis player, a Sunday player, because he worked six, seven days a week generally. But they had these clay courts at the time, which is very unusual to find in the United States now, the clay courts. They had a man specifically maintain them because they had to roll them, water them down, and roll them every day before you could play. So if you come out too early, you couldn't get on until you got done rolling. And so I used to shag balls for my dad on Sundays and play in the clay sand in the background of the metal tennis balls, which they used to have metal. Now it's all plastic. And so getting an early start on the tennis court, I learned to play when I was about 10 years old. My mother used to give me a paper bag lunch. And I used to get on my bike with a couple of my buddies. And we used to go from the north end of town, clear across the big city of about 25, 000, 30, 000 at the time. And we'd wind up at the tennis court. We'd be there all day long, especially in the summer. So I have Early memories also of our family having many picnics there and great reunions. And in fact, my family will have its 100th anniversary here in Boise in 19... 2008. So we're one year behind Julia Davis Park. My grandfather came here and... What was his name? Samuel Koppel. And established a small recycling business across from the Hawaii Hotel, where the big new building is coming up. The Royal Restaurant. Right, I still have the buy-sell agreement that he had with, he purchased from a company in Salt Lake this shed, one horse, one wagon, one scale, $500. So you see things have changed a bit. And all through the years, Judah Davis Park was kind of a central gathering place for Louisians, especially in the summer. And, of course, now there's so many nice parks around. But Julia Davis Park is obviously the grandfather of all parks here. Do you know if your your grandfather knew Mr. Davis? I don't know specifically, but I'm sure being in business that he would probably have known. Because he was a pretty prominent businessman. Oh yes, yes. I never actually heard him speak specifically of him. But my tennis career, starting at the park and relating to present day, they've connected it to the Boise State University, they're going to be able to use the new renovated tennis courts being part of the athletic organization which is a big plus for the tennis world because they'll be able to have national tournaments here. So that's the latest thing on the story of the tennis courts in Julia Davis Park. There's several people that have donated, including Emmett Herndon, part of an old family here, the Davidson family, and several other prominent people have donated. I obviously have donated some funding for the renovation of the Julia Davis Park tennis courts. So we're kind of excited about that, the tennis world. Well tell me, I understand there was a Boise Tennis Club. Yes, the Boise tennis club was the only tennis club in the area for many years until the Boise Swim and Tennis Club was formed in the 60s. But up to that time, the Boise Tennis Club was the active organization for tennis players in the whole southwest Idaho, actually. Do you know who started it? I know Dick DEasum, who was a editor with the Statesman, was very influential. And there was a Don Chapman, who was a banker here. And of course, Dr. John Lundy, the Lundy family was quite active also. Dave Doan was a lawyer here for many years, left and I think he's back as a resident. As a listener. And one of the best things that ever happened to Boise tennis and the tennis world in Boise was when Barbara Chandler moved to Idaho from San Francisco. And I'll never forget the first time I ever had contact with Barbara. She called from Glensbury, Idaho, where her husband had some business. And she said, do you have a women's draw? I said, oh yes. She said, well, I'd like to play in your tennis tournament. So Barbara Chandler was entered and I thought, gee, isn't that nice? They've got some little farm girl from Glensberry, Idaho coming into play. And obviously, she was nationally ranked after I found out about her history. She never lost a tournament, I don't think, in Idaho after that. And she's still active. So the tennis courts, she told me, were the only six public courts in the city when she moved here in the 50s. That's correct. So there were a few private courts in residences. Right, right. But that was actually the only courts in the whole area, a few courts maybe around in Hampton, Caldwell. Basically this was a tennis center. Do you have any memories of the... Did you belong to the tennis club? Your father did? Yes, we both belonged. In 1952, I was the president of the club. And it was active for many years until the Boise Tennis and Swim Club was formed. And I still play at the park occasionally. They have an active group here that's very well organized. Still Boise Tennis Club. Oh, it's still the Boise Tennis Club. Oh, yeah. And I think their organization meets at least on weekends, and maybe more, but they are quite active. Uh-huh. You might want to get the name of... You don't know who... Some way I know, but I may be able to get that name for you. Yeah, that would be fine. Present day. Oh, yeah. And do you remember what the dues were? Barbara told me that she thought they'd charge $5 a year or something. Oh, yeah. It was quite reasonable. $5 a year, and you can have all the tennis you want. They wouldn't furnish the balls though. They wouldn't furnish the balls. That was during the white tennis ball days. Now they're all yellow, of course. Why are they yellow? Well, easier to see. They don't get lost in the background. And we've, even my wife and I, we've had some nice picnics in there at the Julia Davis, haven't we? So tell me about the club. Were they mostly men, or were there men and women? There was men and women, but women weren't as active at the time. There was a couple of very good players here. The tennis players would remember Helen Martin, Helen Martin Jenkins, who I have pictured here. And of course, there are some names here. I can give you Charlotte Graham, who I believe became Perry Silver's wife. They were all high school friends of mine. I think Perry was ahead of the ski area up here in Bogus for several years. Then Patsy Gray, whose father owned a tire shop here, was very active. I'm not sure where his taxi is. Uh-huh. But yeah, it was quite active for men and women. Do you remember, somebody told me there was a quote from Proust, Proust, right by the courts. Do you remember that? Adelia Simplot told me that, which I thought was so interesting. It was a very, you know, a philosophic quote in like a memorial or something. Gosh, I don't know. OK, she said it was somewhere near the tennis courts. Really? She's the only person. It's not there now, though. I don't believe so. So I just wondered if you knew anything. And what about the people that played horseshoes? Were they, that's in that general area. Yeah, that was a little later on. I know they did have the horseshoe pits there and they had their tournaments. That came after? Quite a few years later than when I was active there. And your years being really active were the racket club was built in the 60s? 60s, right. So up till I'd say the late 40s to 60s I was active in the Boise Tennis Club. And you would have tournaments on Memorial Day? Generally there was one state tournament over Labor Day. Yeah, that was the big tournament. And people would come from outside Boise? Not only outside Boise, but the state tournament we had people from Utah and Oregon, usually Portland and Salt Lake, which was a little higher level of competition. Generally, the Boise Southwest Idaho players didn't fare too well if they came in. And you would have awards? Oh yeah, we had a nice awards, we had some picnics obviously out there. Always was so pleasant out there, you know, it's such a gorgeous park. We were very fortunate and fortunate to have this park. So much of the time that you played, you were on clay. Right. And was it Maynard that used to... You have the right name. Maynard was the fellow that maintained it. Maynard, yeah. I've learned lots of things, like Hook was the guy that helped with the Rose Garden, too. Oh, OK. You've got a lot of information. I know all kinds of things. I forgot. That's cute. Yeah, I mean, he was a mainstay there. Without him, it'd be pretty tough. And did you? Were you involved at all in raising the funds to, or was your father to get the, you call them hard courts? Is that what you call hard courts? Right, right. After they resurfaced, because clay is so hard to maintain, it's very costly. So they went to the hard course. I don't recall exactly when. It was after Barbara came. Probably, yeah, it was in the late 50s probably. She told me that MK gave money in the Bureau of Outdoor Recreation. Right. Right. They had the, you know, MK was very instrumental in developing all the parks. I don't know what they would put in the mountains. I haven't came. Do you remember when you were in the late 40s, when you were playing at the tennis courts there on the east end of the park? Do you remember where the pond is now? Was that park then, or was it? Some people told me there was actually garbage landfill going on there. I believe it was just landfill. I don't think That was developed. I can't see that. No, no. A lot of development after the 50s. Uh-huh. Uh-huh. But the zoo was a big, obviously, that was a big attraction. Uh-huh. And You could probably tell me when that was established. I think somebody gave some sort of analog back. They've had like a bird or something there since before 1920, I think. Oh, was there? I mean, but very modest. Yeah, nothing developed into the zoo. Of course, that's a big attraction. Bob Hamill was the architect on that. Do you remember Bob Hamill? Oh, yes. Joe and Bob Hamill. Right. Yes, wonderful. So your children went to the... Oh, they all participated, yeah, we all went for picnics there, family picnics. The kids learned to play tennis. Most of my family played there. Was there... Let's see... How long in the 60s were you there? Because in the 60s they had the hippies that came to the park, but I don't think they were at that end of the park. I never, I never realized that if there was. I think they were by the band shell. They must have been hidden by the band shell or something. Yeah. So what did you wear to play? What's it like? Well, as in the pictures, there actually isn't too much different other than, of course, the women have more attractive clothing now, but the men's really hasn't changed that much, you know, t-shirts and shorts. Here's a Of course I have a picture Take note White clothes, they were white? Generally, yeah, white was the order of the day. Of course, now it's hard to find anybody in Hawaii. Yeah. And I guess the schools started playing, because you didn't learn in school that, did you have tennis in school? Actually in the early years when I just began, when I was 10, 12 years old, there was none, but when I got to high school. You went to Boise High? Oh yes. My dad went there and my kids all went there. So we got a lot of alumni at Boise High. But at Boise High you had tennis. Oh yes. Did you play there at the Julia Davis courts? Uh-huh, yeah, that was the center. Although the last year or so they put in some courts where East Junior High is, And so they were using those courts also. But that was basically where all the high school, we played Caldwell, Nampa. It was basically the competition. Twin Falls. And were there any... It was... This was... The tennis club was a total volunteer. We didn't have anybody, but... Right. You know, everybody was volunteered. They had the treasurer, obviously, secretary, president. There was all this folks that were fans of tennis, enthusiasts. Do you know, besides your father, can you think of some of the, I guess you've told me some of the people that. Oh, of his era? Yeah. Yeah, Dick DeEasum. And there was a fellow he played with every Sunday's name, Wynn Countryman. He was an insurance man here. At one tournament They had a headline because they won the first round that had Koppel and Countryman win first round. I don't think they got any farther than that, but they were the locals. So they got all the publicity. Yeah, my dad always laughed about that one. Poplar and countrymen. I think there might be some countrymen left. We have a couple sons. I believe they're still residents of Boise. Did you ever go to the band show? I've been there not too often. It's at the other end. Yeah, Yeah, occasionally. When the kids were young, of course, we went quite often. Piglets, the zoo, they had kind of a carnival set up when we married around. And it was great for the kids. Right set up with merry-go-round that's great for the kids they don't even Have a partner She's a Weiser girl. A Weiser girl. Yeah. Tennis was a focal point. Julia Davis Park for many years. The tennis in all of southwest Idaho, I think you could say. And now I'm really happy to hear that it's going to be connected with the college, because It's going to upgrade it and continue, continue the part of the Julia Davis Park as part of the tennis community. It's really wonderful. It's a great environment, you know. It's beautiful. Right. Do you know how your father started playing? That I don't. I just wonder who got the first chords going. Yeah, I don't know. Was your father born in Denver? My father was born in Denver he was about 10 eight I'm just a year behind you right So your father came here at 10. And so you don't know how he learned to play tennis. I really don't. I'm just curious who got the skill. We never kind of discussed how he got into it. He actually wanted to be a lawyer. He was in recycling. Yeah. He was ahead of his time. Right. My grandfather who was in business was killed in a truck accident in 1918 when my dad graduated high school. He went up to University of Idaho and he was only there one day. Your father? Yeah, he was called back to head up the business. He was the eldest one. He'd lost the older brother in the war. World War I. Right, so he was next in line. So he became a businessman. I remember. I knew him, I believe. Oh, yeah. He was quite active in the metal theater. You were, too, weren't you? No, no. But mine's, no. But I used to go. Yeah. I remember I used to see you there, right? Mm-hmm. Yeah, we enjoyed that. It's interesting. It'd be interesting. Do you think you could, would there be any papers or anything of your family that would help tell how he, because there were almost no courts. I mean it's like how do you learn to swim if there's no swimming pools? Right, right. So how did he... You know, I just don't know how he started tennis. It must be a family secret. Well, I'd really like to know how they got those. Play courts were quite expensive, well they're at least expensive to maintain. Yeah, I don't know how that came about or who even, you know, if MK had anything to do with putting him in, he might be able to delve through their research. Yeah, yeah. But he didn't leave any history, written history? No, Nothing at all about his high school, other than he was a debater. He was not an athlete. He was a champion debater. Oh, really? What would have happened if he'd gone? Yeah, what would have happened, you know, isn't that something? If he would have become a lawyer. Yeah. Fate steps in. Right. So one day at the University of Idaho. Right. Then my grandmother stayed, of course she was here. I have had an uncle, Simon, who was affiliated for a while, then he went on his own. In a business? Yes. What was his last name? Koppel. That was my father's youngest brother. His oldest brother's the one that died in the war. Uh-huh. So yeah, they had a lot of family there. One time I had, oh my God, I must have had 30, 40 relatives in Boise. Is that right? Yeah. We were related to the Sarlet family who had the men's wardrobe. I don't know if you remember that. Uh-huh. Uh-huh. Lots of cousins. Now I'm the only one left. Is that right? Yeah, All my kids are out of state. Are they? Oh. Oh. Oh. Oh. They doing well? Pretty good. They got a little health problem. Any of them tennis players? They all were tennis players, yeah. Yeah, unfortunately, one boy has MS, and he was a really good tennis player, so that's tough on him. So how would, when you were growing up, was Boise seen as sort of, considering it was a fairly small community, was it seen as active in tennis as opposed to other towns that have that size? Right. It was probably the hub of Idaho tennis, I'd say, in the whole state when June went. I mean, you came over here as a high schooler, and came over for tournaments. Uh-huh. But there's a big bond. I'm not part of his team. This was the hub, though. I mean, you played tennis in high school. Do you Want to introduce your wife to the tape recorder? Oh, I'd be delighted. My wife Paula, Reinhaus, Koppel. How do you spell that? R-E-I-N-H-A-E-S They're an old pioneer family from Weezer. How long your family were there in the 1890s? Oh, way down. So we've seen a lot of Changes. A lot of changes in Idaho. Mostly good. Few too many people, maybe. Right, right. I think a lot of the small towns in Idaho were great tennis. Were they? Oh, yes. Yeah, they had high school tennis. Every place. But this was actually the center where you'd come for the, you know, the state tournaments and the district tournaments and stuff. Paula, you play tennis? Is that how you two met? Actually we met at Boise Little Theater. We're both worth trying out for parts and neither one of us got the part, but we got each other. OK. OK. Good. Well, is there anything else that you think that people living in, well, just about the park, or about the tennis club? I guess there were lights after that you got it hard-surfaced. Yes, that was a big improvement obviously because so many people had to wait till afterward to play and they didn't have time. So this opened up a whole new era, actually. So that was a great, great improvement. And now we have tennis courts all over the town. It's a very active, athletic organization. How could they have named that young? What? Coaches in school, in high school. Oh, yeah. That's kind of interesting. They might. I don't know if they're still alive. Did you hear the name of Cecil Pollard? I've heard that name. He was a chemistry teacher at Boise High for many years. Most of the tennis players that went to Boise High will remember Cecil Pollard. He was a chemistry teacher? Yes. And he didn't coach? Actually, yeah. They didn't have specific coaches in for anything. They usually pulled them out of some teacher that knew a little about it. He had never played tennis in his life, but I'll never forget his one comment. He says, remember, it's a game of angles. So that was pretty specific. He knew his strategies. He was a great guy. And then in college, you went to Boise Junior College. You went to Boise J.C. Right. And kind of an interesting thing about that area.

lines.line: # Introduction and Early Memories

> Marilyn Shuler introduces herself and Howard Kopple. Howard talks about his birthplace, Boise, and shares fond early memories of Julia Davis Park, especially watching his father play tennis there on clay courts.
** introduction, early memories, Boise, Julia Davis Park, tennis, clay courts **
keywords: ** introduction, early memories, Boise, Julia Davis Park, tennis, clay courts **

lines.line: # Tennis Beginnings

> Howard describes spending childhood days at the park, learning to play tennis, and transitioning from watching his father to playing himself. He recalls biking to the courts and spending full days there during summers.
** childhood, learning tennis, biking, summer days, paper bag lunch **
keywords: ** childhood, learning tennis, biking, summer days, paper bag lunch **

lines.line: # Family and Community Impact

> Howard discusses his family's long history in Boise, including when they would picnic in the park and celebrate family anniversaries. He shares his grandfather's business ventures and the community's connection to Julia Davis Park.
** family history, anniversaries, picnics, community, business ventures **
keywords: ** family history, anniversaries, picnics, community, business ventures **

lines.line: # Link to Present Day Tennis Developments

> Howard connects his early tennis experiences with present developments linked to Boise State University and the renovations happening at Julia Davis Park. He discusses donors involved in the renovations and the integration with university athletics.
** tennis developments, Boise State University, renovations, donors, athletic integration **
keywords: ** tennis developments, Boise State University, renovations, donors, athletic integration **

lines.line: # History of the Boise Tennis Club

> Howard talks about the Boise Tennis Club, sharing its origins, notable members, and how it served the region before the Boise Swim and Tennis Club was established. He mentions influential figures who shaped the club's history.
** Boise Tennis Club, origins, notable members, influential figures, Swim and Tennis Club **

lines.line: # Tournaments and Events

> Howard reminisces about the tournaments held at the club, including state tournaments that attracted players from across the region. He reflects on the local players' performances and the camaraderie established through these events.
** tournaments, state events, camaraderie, performance, local players **

lines.line: # Evolution of Tennis and Infrastructure

> Discussing the evolution from clay to hard courts, Howard acknowledges the people who maintained the first courts and how the infrastructure has changed, including the introduction of lights for nighttime play.
** evolution, clay courts, hard courts, infrastructure, nighttime play **
keywords: ** evolution, clay courts, hard courts, infrastructure, nighttime play **

> Howard shares more personal history about his father and his unexpected path into business over law, revealing how family and historical circumstances shaped career directions. He mentions his extended family and their roles in the community.
** family legacy, personal history, career paths, historical circumstances, community roles **

story.description: Recorded on March 2, 2007. Also interviewed is Koppel's wife, Paula.
